{{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|23|N|137|05|07|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Nishio, Aichi]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[Yahagi River]] facing [[Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html Aichi Prefecture Cultural Properties ] *[http://nishiokanko.com/list/shop/syouhoujikofun Nishio tourist information site] *[https://www.city.nishio.aichi.jp/index.cfm/8,14704,91,408,html Nishio City official site]

The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|23|N|137|05|07|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Nishio, Aichi]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[Yahagi River]] facing [[Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|23|N|137|05|07|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Nishio, Aichi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[Yahagi River]] facing [[Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html Aichi Prefecture Cultural Properties ] *[http://nishiokanko.com/list/shop/syouhoujikofun Nishio tourist information site] *[https://www.city.nishio.aichi.jp/index.cfm/8,14704,91,408,html Nishio City official site]

{{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|23|N|137|05|07|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Nishio, Aichi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[Yahagi River]] facing [[Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html Aichi Prefecture Cultural Properties ] *[http://nishiokanko.com/list/shop/syouhoujikofun Nishio tourist information site] *[https://www.city.nishio.aichi.jp/index.cfm/8,14704,91,408,html Nishio City official site]

{{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|23|N|137|05|07|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Nishio, Aichi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[Yahagi River]] facing [[:en:Mikawa Bay|Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html Aichi Prefecture Cultural Properties ] *[http://nishiokanko.com/list/shop/syouhoujikofun Nishio tourist information site] *[https://www.city.nishio.aichi.jp/index.cfm/8,14704,91,408,html Nishio City official site]

{{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|47|23|N|137|05|07|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[:en:Yahagi River|Yahagi River]] facing [[:en:Mikawa Bay|Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html Aichi Prefecture Cultural Properties ] *[http://nishiokanko.com/list/shop/syouhoujikofun Nishio tourist information site] *[https://www.city.nishio.aichi.jp/index.cfm/8,14704,91,408,html Nishio City official site]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24867326]] Wikidata name: Shōbōji Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.789444444444|N|137.08555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Nishio|qid=Q317844}} | region = [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[:en:Yahagi River|Yahagi River]] facing [[:en:Mikawa Bay|Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html Aichi Prefecture Cultural Properties ] *[http://nishiokanko.com/list/shop/syouhoujikofun Nishio tourist information site] *[https://www.city.nishio.aichi.jp/index.cfm/8,14704,91,408,html Nishio City official site]

Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[:en:Yahagi River|Yahagi River]] facing [[:en:Mikawa Bay|Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shoboji Kofun (Nishio)]][[en:Shōbōji Kofun]][[ja:正法寺古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24867326]] Wikidata name: Shōbōji Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.789444444444|N|137.08555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Nishio|WD=Q317844}} | region = [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[:en:Yahagi River|Yahagi River]] facing [[:en:Mikawa Bay|Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline}} *[https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html Aichi Prefecture Cultural Properties ] *[http://nishiokanko.com/list/shop/syouhoujikofun Nishio tourist information site] *[https://www.city.nishio.aichi.jp/index.cfm/8,14704,91,408,html Nishio City official site]

Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shoboji Kofun (Nishio)]][[en:Shōbōji Kofun]][[ja:正法寺古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24867326]] Wikidata name: Shōbōji Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.789444444444|N|137.08555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Nishio|WD=Q317844}} | region = [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[:en:Yahagi River|Yahagi River]] facing [[:en:Mikawa Bay|Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]

The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.

The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shoboji Kofun (Nishio)]][[en:Shōbōji Kofun]][[ja:正法寺古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shoboji Kofun (Nishio)]][[en:Shōbōji Kofun]][[ja:正法寺古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shoboji Kofun (Nishio)]][[en:Shōbōji Kofun]][[ja:正法寺古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24867326]] Wikidata name: Shōbōji Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun in Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Shōbōji Kofun | native_name =正法寺古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 160227 Whole view of Shoboji Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_type = Japan Aichi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbōji Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.789444444444|N|137.08555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Nishio|WD=Q317844}} | region = [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th to early 5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Shōbōji Kofun'''|正法寺古墳||}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the [[:en:Kira, Aichi|Kira]] neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nishio, Aichi|Nishio, Aichi]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1936, with the area under protection extended in 2004.<ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/syubetsu/siseki.html|title=Aichi Cultural Properties Navi|language=Japanese |publisher=Aichi Prefecture] |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1429|title=正法寺古墳|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Shōbōji Kofun is located near the mouth of the [[:en:Yahagi River|Yahagi River]] facing [[:en:Mikawa Bay|Mikawa Bay]] in central Aichi Prefecture, on what was once a peninsula that extended about 400 meters east from an independent hill at an elevation of 115 meters. The coastline during the Kofun period is estimated to have reached about ten kilometers inland from the present site; however, from ancient and medieval times [[:en:Land reclamation|Land reclamation]] gradually progressed due to sedimentation of the Yahagi River. The hills within two kilometers around the old Kofun are formed of [[:en:gabbro|gabbro]], and the stones covering the ''kofun'' are almost entirely gabbroic. The soil around the tumulus is reddish brown clay, which is due to the iron content of gabbro.
The name of "Shōbōji" refers to a [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] sect temple built by the [[:en:Ashikaga clan|Ashikaga clan]] in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] and converted to the [[Sōtō]] sect during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The tumulus was covered in pine forest during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and was not recognized as a ''kofun'' until after the start of the twentieth century. It was designated as a park in the 1930s, but as severely damaged by the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]] in 1959. The site was abandoned and overgrown for many years until the park was revived in 1989 and the dimensions of the ''kofun'' surveyed. Although the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] was not opened, a total of 11 trenches were [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] in its sides from 1985 to 2002.
Per the 2001 and 2002 excavation, the tumulus was established to be a three-tiered {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. However, this tumulus is unusual in that the front line is oblique to the main axis. This lack of symmetry was due to the contour of a natural hill, which was incorporated into the tumulus. The first and second ties were formed by cutting and contouring the natural hill, and the uppermost level was added as an embankment. The total length of the tumulus is 94 meters. The anterior rectangular portion is 55 meters wide and 6 meters high, and then posterior circular portion has diameter of 62 meters and height of eight meters. The mound was covered with ''[[fukiishi]]'' made from natural stone split stones found in the vicinity, rather than rounded river stones which was more typical. Numerous cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been uncovered at the site (although none in situ, and none with any decoration) as well as numerous [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery shards. The ''haniwa'' date the construction of the tumulus to the late 4th to early 5th century.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Currently, the tumulus is maintained as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]]. It is located about five minutes by car from [[:en:Kira-Yoshida Station|Kira-Yoshida Station]] on the [[:en:Meitetsu Nishio Line|Meitetsu Nishio Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Aichi)]]
